title: Pituitary Apoplexy
oneLiner: Sudden haemorrhagic infarction of a pituitary adenoma causing headache, vision loss, adrenal crisis; endocrine emergency with high morbidity if missed.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 45-year-old overweight man with known acromegaly (or undiagnosed) collapses in the clinic waiting room with the worst headache of his life. He is nauseated and photophobic. On examination, he has a left-sided ptosis, a fixed and dilated left pupil, and limited upward gaze on the left. His blood pressure is 85/55 mmHg with a heart rate of 110 bpm. He looks pale and sweaty. You check his GCS – it's 14 (eye opening to voice, confused). His wife says he missed his last two clinic appointments and has been tired and weak for weeks.

**Dissect the Pattern:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Known pituitary adenoma (acromegaly) | The underlying substrate – most apoplexy occurs in a pre-existing adenoma (often null-cell or non-functioning, but any type can bleed) |
| Sudden severe headache ("worst ever") | Subarachnoid or meningeal irritation from blood in the sellar region and surrounding cisterns |
| Ptosis, fixed dilated pupil, limited upward gaze | **Cavernous sinus syndrome** – third nerve palsy (pupil-involved) due to lateral expansion of the haemorrhage into the cavernous sinus; also can involve IV and VI |
| Hypotension + tachycardia + pallor + sweating | **Adrenal crisis** – acute ACTH deficiency causes haemodynamic shock; this is the life-threatening component |
| Fatigue and weakness in recent weeks | Chronic hypopituitarism (probably ACTH and TSH deficiency) that has been building – the apoplexy then unmasks acute decompensation |
| Obesity + acromegaly | Suggests pre-existing endocrine pathology that may have been poorly controlled or undiagnosed |

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Isolated ophthalmoplegia without headache:** A painless third nerve palsy with pupil involvement (no subarachnoid bleeding, only cavernous sinus compression). Can be mistaken for a posterior communicating artery aneurysm if you don't check for hypotension.
- **Nonspecific collapse with encephalopathy:** GCS 8, no localising signs. The hypotension is treated with fluids but they don't improve. You forget to check cortisol and give steroids – patient crashes when you start thyroid hormone replacement (untreated adrenal insufficiency ➜ precipitates adrenal crisis).
- **Fever + neck stiffness + photophobia:** Looks like meningitis or subarachnoid haemorrhage. Lumbar puncture shows xanthochromia due to blood breakdown products. But the real clue is the low sodium (hyponatraemia from secondary adrenal insufficiency or SIADH) and hypotension unresponsive to antibiotics.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Pituitary apoplexy is a spontaneous haemorrhage or infarction into a pituitary adenoma (usually a previously undiagnosed tumour). It causes rapid swelling of the pituitary gland within the bony sella turcica, compressing surrounding structures – the optic chiasm, cranial nerves in the cavernous sinus, and the normal pituitary tissue. The sudden loss of ACTH production precipitates a life-threatening adrenal crisis.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**  
This is a **“can't miss”** diagnosis. It is uncommon but carries a mortality of up to 10–15% if unrecognised, and survivors suffer permanent hypopituitarism. The major pitfall is that the headache is often labelled as migraine or subarachnoid haemorrhage, and the hypotension is attributed to sepsis or GI bleed. **Giving steroids early (hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat) before any other hormone replacement is the single most important intervention.** Also, urgent neurosurgical decompression can salvage vision if performed within 48–72 hours. The stakes: blindness, coma, and death from adrenal crisis.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story of How It Develops:**  
Pituitary adenomas are highly vascular but have a fragile blood supply – they outgrow their own blood vessels, leaving a tumour that is prone to ischaemic necrosis and subsequent haemorrhage. Any increase in intrasellar pressure (e.g., from tumour growth, blood pressure spikes, anticoagulation) can trigger rupture of these fragile vessels. The tumour swells rapidly, compressing the surrounding normal pituitary gland and its stalk. This sudden loss of ACTH (and often TSH) produces an acute state of hypopituitarism, most critically loss of cortisol production.

**Mechanism → Sign:**

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|-----------|---------------|
| Haemorrhage into tumour → sudden sella expansion + irritation of meninges | **Severe headache** (worse than any previous, often occipital or retro-orbital) – from meningeal irritation (similar to SAH) |
| Compression of optic chiasm | **Bitemporal hemianopia** – classically with a central scotoma; can progress to blindness |
| Lateral expansion into cavernous sinus → cranial nerves III, IV, V1, V2, VI compromised | **Ptosis, mydriasis, upward gaze palsy** (III), **ophthalmoplegia** (IV, VI), **periorbital numbness** (V1, V2) |
| Loss of ACTH → acute secondary adrenal insufficiency | **Hypotension, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, shock** – cortisol absent means no permissive effect on catecholamines, no gluconeogenesis, no sodium retention |
| Loss of TSH → acute secondary hypothyroidism | **Fatigue, cold intolerance, bradycardia** – but less immediate because T4 has long half-life; however, giving T4 without cortisol can worsen adrenal crisis |
| Possible compression of hypothalamic-pituitary stalk → loss of ADH | **Diabetes insipidus** (polyuria, polydipsia, hypernatraemia) – less common but can be missed |
| Blood in subarachnoid space (if tumour erodes through diaphragm sella) | **Neck stiffness, photophobia, altered consciousness** – mimics SAH |

**Connecting to Pattern Recognition Cues:**  
- The **hypotension** in the vignette is not cardiogenic – it's from acute adrenal crisis. The clue is that it doesn't respond to fluids alone.  
- The **ptosis + mydriasis** localises to CN III in the cavernous sinus; the pupil is involved because parasympathetic fibres run on the outside of the nerve and are compressed early.  
- The **fatigue and weakness** prodrome reflects chronic hypopituitarism – the apoplexy is the acute-on-chronic decompensation.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
Most cases are **spontaneous** in a previously undiagnosed adenoma (often null-cell or prolactinoma). But there are identifiable triggers:

| Category | Trigger / Cause | Stem Clue That Points to It |
|----------|----------------|-----------------------------|
| **Most common** | Spontaneous haemorrhage into pre-existing adenoma (often non-functioning) | Patient with no known diagnosis, presents with headache + visual loss; no trigger found |
| **Iatrogenic** | Anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs, heparin) | Patient on blood thinners for AF, VTE, or mechanical valve; sudden headache |
| **Iatrogenic** | Surgery (e.g., hip replacement, CABG) – hypotension + bleeding tendency | Post-operative day 1–3, patient develops headache and hypotension; may be attributed to anaesthetic or fluid shift |
| **Endocrine** | Pregnancy (Sheehan's syndrome variant) – in a gravid woman with known pituitary adenoma | Pregnant woman with headache and hypotension; rare but possible |
| **Drug-induced** | Dopamine agonists (bromocriptine, cabergoline) – can cause tumour necrosis | Known prolactinoma treated with dopamine agonist; rapid shrinkage can precipitate apoplexy |
| **Trauma** | Head injury with direct pituitary trauma | Patient with recent MVA or fall; may have other skull base fractures |
| **Radiation** | Radiotherapy to sellar region (e.g., for tumour) | Headache months after pituitary radiation |
| **Infectious / Inflammatory** | Very rare – can be associated with polyarteritis nodosa, SLE, or acute infection | Unusual context; look for systemic vasculitis features |

**Highlight the most common cause:** Spontaneous haemorrhage into a preexisting non-functioning pituitary adenoma.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria:**  
There is no formal consensus scoring system, but a clinical diagnosis is based on **sudden onset severe headache + visual disturbance + features of hypopituitarism** combined with imaging confirmation.

**Key Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Urgent non-contrast CT head (sella protocol)** | Hyperdense mass in the sella (acute haemorrhage) with possible extension into suprasellar cistern or cavernous sinus | First-line imaging – fast, widely available, can show acute haemorrhage as high density | **Positive:** hyperdensity in sella confirms haemorrhage. **Negative does not rule out** – infarct without haemorrhage may be isodense. Need MRI. |
| **MRI brain with contrast (pituitary protocol)** | T1 hyperintensity in acute/subacute haemorrhage; T2 hypointensity; may show tumour + fluid levels; contrast enhancement of viable tumour | Gold standard – better for differentiating haemorrhage vs infarction, and for surgical planning | **Single finding that confirms:** Heterogeneous mass in sella with evidence of recent haemorrhage and compression of optic chiasm. |
| **Serum cortisol (random, or 8am)** | Low cortisol (< 100 nmol/L or < 3.6 μg/dL) in acute setting | Diagnose adrenal crisis – do NOT wait for synacthen test; treat empirically | **Critical:** a low cortisol in a hypotensive patient with headache is diagnostic of adrenal insufficiency. **Do not delay steroids for this result** – give hydrocortisone first. |
| **Serum ACTH** | Low or inappropriately normal (should be high in response to low cortisol) | Distinguish primary from secondary adrenal insufficiency | Low ACTH + low cortisol = secondary (pituitary) cause. |
| **Serum prolactin** | Highly elevated in stalk compression (disconnection); or low in pituitary destruction | Helps differentiate apoplexy from prolactinoma – if prolactin > 5000 mIU/L, suggests prolactinoma with apoplexy (medical treatment may be preferred) | **Stalk effect:** moderate elevation (1000–3000). > 5000 suggests pure prolactinoma. |
| **Full pituitary panel** | LH, FSH, TSH, free T4, GH, IGF-1 | Assess baseline deficits for long-term replacement | May be low or low-normal; insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) may be low if GH deficient. |
| **Electrolytes** | Hyponatraemia (low Na < 135) | Adrenal insufficiency causes impaired free water excretion | Moderate to severe hyponatraemia (Na 115–130) is common. |
| **Blood glucose** | Hypoglycaemia (low glucose < 3.3 mmol/L) | Adrenal insufficiency causes fasting hypoglycaemia | Can be severe; gives clue to adrenal crisis in a collapsed patient. |
| **Coagulation screen** | PT/APTT/INR if on anticoagulants | Identify iatrogenic trigger | Elevated INR if warfarin; normal in spontaneous cases. |

**Pattern-Recognition: What Single Finding Confirms / Rules Out?**

- **Confirms:** MRI showing intrasellar haemorrhage with optic chiasm compression + low random cortisol in a shocked patient.
- **Rules out:** Normal cortisol (> 500 nmol/L) in a hypotensive patient makes adrenal crisis unlikely (but does not rule out apoplexy itself – could have infarct without complete ACTH loss; still need imaging).

**Note on Lumbar Puncture:**  
Only perform if CT negative and you suspect SAH. In apoplexy, CSF may be xanthochromic or bloody, but this is not specific. Do not tap if there is suspicion of raised ICP (focal signs, altered GCS) – risk of herniation.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour):**

1. **Airway, Breathing, Circulation** – High-flow O₂, two large-bore IVs. If GCS ≤ 8, intubate.
2. **Bloods** – Random cortisol, ACTH, prolactin, full pituitary profile, U&Es, glucose, coagulation, FBC.
3. **Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV STAT** – This is the single most important drug. Do not wait for cortisol result. In adrenal crisis, cortisol deficiency is immediately life-threatening.
4. **Fluid resuscitation** – Start with 0.9% saline (or dextrose saline if hypoglycaemic). Avoid hypotonic fluids (worsens hyponatraemia).
5. **Correct hypoglycaemia** – If glucose < 3.3, give 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV, then 5% dextrose infusion.
6. **Stat CT head** (sella protocol) – to identify haemorrhage and plan for neurosurgery.
7. **Discuss with neurosurgery** – Even if you aren't sure of the diagnosis, any suspected apoplexy warrants neurosurgical consultation.

**What NOT To Do:**
- **Do not give levothyroxine before hydrocortisone** – this can precipitate adrenal crisis because T4 increases metabolic clearance of cortisol.
- **Do not give desmopressin for hyponatraemia** – the hyponatraemia is from adrenal insufficiency, not SIADH. Steroids will correct it.
- **Do not give mannitol for raised ICP** – unless confirmed by CT; steroids may help.

**Ward (24–72 Hours):**

- **Hydrocortisone 50 mg IV QDS** (or 100 mg TDS) – continue as stress dose until stable, then taper to oral replacement.
- **Monitor:**
  - **Vital signs q15 min** – BP, HR, GCS, pupils.
  - **Visual fields and acuity daily** – documented by neurosurgery or ophthalmology.
  - **Serum sodium and glucose q6h** – to guide fluids and dextrose.
- **MRI pituitary** – within 24 hours for definitive diagnosis and surgical planning.
- **Neurosurgical decompression (transsphenoidal)** – indicated if:
  - **Progressive visual loss** (not improving with steroids)
  - **Severe ophthalmoplegia** with no improvement
  - **Coma or decreasing GCS** due to mass effect
  - **Large haemorrhage** compressing hypothalamus
- **If no surgery** (e.g., mild symptoms, improving vision), continue steroids and observe with daily MRI.
- **Thyroid replacement** – only start after 24–48 hours of adequate steroid therapy. Start levothyroxine 50–100 mcg PO/NG daily (low dose to avoid metabolic crisis).

**Long-Term:**

- **Check full pituitary function at 6–12 weeks** – many patients develop permanent hypopituitarism.
- **Lifelong replacement:**
  - Hydrocortisone 10–20 mg PO daily in divided doses (e.g., 10 mg on waking, 5 mg at 4 pm)
  - Levothyroxine 75–150 mcg daily (titrate to free T4 in upper normal range)
  - Sex hormone replacement if needed (testosterone / oestrogen)
  - Growth hormone (if deficient and no contraindication) after 1 year
- **Diabetes insipidus** – if present, treat with desmopressin (DDAVP) 100–200 mcg PO TDS or 1–2 mcg SC.
- **Follow-up imaging** – MRI at 3, 6, 12 months then yearly for 3 years to monitor for tumour regrowth.
- **Sick day rules** – corticosteroid dose doubling during illness to prevent adrenal crisis (teach patient and family).

**Drug Doses Inline:**

- Hydrocortisone: 100 mg IV stat, then 50 mg IV QDS for 24–72h, then oral taper.
- Levothyroxine: 50–100 mcg PO daily, start after 24h of steroids.
- Desmopressin: 100–200 mcg PO TDS (or 1–2 mcg SC/IV) as needed for DI.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Pituitary Apoplexy | Subarachnoid Haemorrhage (SAH) | Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis | Meningitis | Migraine (Severe) |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------|------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | Sudden, severe, retro-orbital or occipital | Thunderclap headache (worst ever), often occipital/nuchal | Gradual onset, periorbital pain with redness | Diffuse, with neck pain | Unilateral, throbbing, may have aura |
| **Exam** | Ptosis, mydriasis, abducens palsy; bitemporal hemianopia; hypotension; pallor | Neck stiffness, photophobia, focal signs less common (unless aneurysm compressing CN III – then pupil usually dilated) | Proptosis, chemosis, periorbital oedema; CN III, IV, VI palsy; fever; no hypotension | Fever, meningism, altered GCS, rash (N. meningitidis) | May have photophobia, but no focal signs; no hypotension |
| **ECG** | Non-specific (may show sinus tachycardia from shock) | May show ST-T changes due to neurogenic stunned myocardium | Sinus tachycardia from sepsis | Tachycardia from fever | Normal |
| **Labs** | Low cortisol, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia; prolactin may be high or low; coagulopathy if on anticoagulants | Xanthochromia / blood on LP; normal cortisol | ↑ WCC, ↑ CRP, may have positive blood cultures | ↑ WCC, ↑ CRP; LP shows pleocytosis and low glucose | Normal |
| **Key differentiating feature** | **Low cortisol + hypotension + visual field defect** | **LP with xanthochromia** + thunderclap headache without endocrine features | **Fever + proptosis + chemosis** + cavernous sinus signs; no adrenal crisis | **Fever + meningism**; no ocular motor palsy unless advanced; normal cortisol | **Recurrent pattern**; normal exam and labs between attacks; no visual field loss |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Adrenal crisis with cardiovascular collapse** | Profound hypotension unresponsive to fluids, hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, lethargy → coma | BP < 90/60, HR > 120, GCS < 12, glucose < 3.3, Na < 130 | Give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat; fluids; treat cause. Move to ICU. |
| **Permanent visual loss** | Progressive bitemporal hemianopia → blindness over hours to days | No light perception; optic atrophy develops after 2 weeks if untreated | Urgent neurosurgical decompression. Vision can be saved if treated within 48–72 hours. |
| **Permanent cranial nerve palsy** | Persistent ptosis, diplopia, ophthalmoplegia | CN III, IV, VI deficits persist beyond 6–12 months | Decompression may help if early; later – squint surgery. |
| **Permanent panhypopituitarism** | Lifelong dependence on corticosteroids, thyroid, sex hormones, GH | Fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, cold intolerance, infertility, loss of libido | Lifelong hormone replacement; teach sick day rules; endocrine follow-up. |
| **Diabetes insipidus** | Polyuria > 3 L/day, polydipsia, hypernatraemia (Na > 145) | Urine output > 200 mL/h, thirst, high serum osmolality | Desmopressin; monitor Na carefully; may be temporary or permanent. |
| **Recurrent apoplexy** | Regrowth of tumour with repeat haemorrhage | Same presentation weeks to months later | Consider radiotherapy if residual tumour; long-term MRI surveillance. |

**Late Presentation Pattern (What Happens If Untreated):**  
A patient found unconscious with fixed dilated pupils bilaterally, no eye movements, and refractory hypotension. They may have had a mild headache days ago that was ignored. Their GCS is 3, they are ventilated, and CT shows massive sella haematoma with hydrocephalus. Prognosis is grim – they likely will not regain consciousness and may die from brain herniation.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **PITUITARY APOPLEXY** (for key features)  
   **P** – Pupil (CN III palsy with mydriasis)  
   **I** – Intense headache  
   **T** – TSH / ACTH loss (adrenal and thyroid)  
   **U** – Unconsciousness / altered GCS  
   **I** – Ischaemic infarction of adenoma  
   **T** – Temporal field loss  
   **A** – Adrenal crisis (low cortisol)  
   **R** – Radiological (CT/MRI)  
   **Y** – Yield to steroids early  

   **A** – ACTH (low)  
   **P** – Prolactin (stalk effect)  
   **O** – Ocular palsies  
   **P** – Pituitary tumour  
   **L** – Low sodium  
   **E** – Emergency steroid  
   **X** – X-ray (CT)  
   **Y** – Yes, call neurosurgeon

2. **"Headache + Eye Signs + Shock"** — the triad you must never forget.

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**

- **Rule of thumb for hypotension:** If a patient with severe headache has BP < 90/60, check a random cortisol and give steroids before doing anything else.
- **Visual fields:** Apoplexy classically causes bitemporal hemianopia, but sometimes a central scotoma is the first sign – test red desaturation (a red pin disappears in the affected field).
- **Lumbar puncture crux:** If you suspect SAH but CT is negative, don't forget to check cortisol and prolactin before LP – a single low cortisol in a shocked patient is diagnostic.
- **The mimic of 'sepsis':** Pituitary apoplexy can present with fever and tachycardia – don't be fooled into treating as sepsis without giving steroids.
- **Don't start thyroxine:** Until you have given hydrocortisone – this is a classic trap in ICU.
- **Prolactin level:** A very high prolactin (> 5000 mIU/L) suggests a prolactinoma; if the patient has apoplexy, medical therapy (bromocriptine) may be first line instead of surgery, but always discuss with endocrinology.
- **Pregnancy:** In a pregnant woman with headache and hypotension, consider Sheehan's syndrome (postpartum infarction) – but apoplexy can occur in any trimester if there is an adenoma.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**

- Giving IV levothyroxine before steroids (precipitates hypotension)
- Attributing hypotension to sepsis without checking cortisol
- Ordering lumbar puncture when there is a known sellar mass (risk of coning)
- Using desmopressin for hyponatraemia without first correcting cortisol deficiency
- Discharging a patient with mild apoplexy without endocrine follow-up (they will relapse)

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to Call Senior / ICU / Neurosurgery:**

| Condition | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Hypotension unresponsive to 500 mL fluid bolus | BP < 90/60 after 1L saline | Call senior (registrar or consultant) and prepare ICU transfer – may need vasopressors. |
| GCS < 13 or falling by ≥ 2 points in 30 minutes | GCS 8 or below = consider intubation | Call crash team / anaesthetics; intubate for airway protection and CT head. |
| New focal neurological deficit (visual loss, CN palsy) | Any visual field loss or new fixed dilated pupil | Urgent neurosurgery consult – may need decompression within hours. |
| Severe hyponatraemia (Na < 120) with seizures | Na < 120 or seizure | Call ICU; give hypertonic saline (3%) 100 mL over 10 minutes if seizure, then endocrinology input. |
| Hypoglycaemia (glucose < 2.2) not correcting with 50% dextrose | Persistent hypoglycaemia after 2 ampules | Add hydrocortisone if not already given; inform senior. |
| Signs of transtentorial herniation (unequal pupils, decerebrate posturing, Cushing's reflex) | Any one of these | Crash call – emergent neurosurgery; consider mannitol (but steroids first); prepare for theatre. |
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥ 7 (especially if component for consciousness) | Escalate to critical care outreach; organise CT. |

**Specific Thresholds to Document:**

- Δ GCS ≥ 2 from baseline
- SBP < 90 mmHg despite 1L crystalloid
- Lactate > 4 mmol/L
- Na < 125 or > 150
- Glucose < 3.0 mmol/L
- PaO₂ < 8 kPa on air (or SpO₂ < 92% on 15 L O₂)
- Any seizure

**Remember:** If you suspect pituitary apoplexy, **call the neurosurgery registrar and the endocrinology registrar immediately** – even before confirmatory imaging. This is a time-critical joint medical–surgical emergency.

---

*End of Study-Mode Entry – Review this page before your next shift. Add your own notes below if you encounter a real case.*

**One-liner:** Sudden haemorrhagic infarction of a pituitary adenoma causing headache, vision loss, adrenal crisis; endocrine emergency with high morbidity if missed.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 45-year-old overweight man with known acromegaly (or undiagnosed) collapses in the clinic waiting room with the worst headache of his life. He is nauseated and photophobic. On examination, he has a left-sided ptosis, a fixed and dilated left pupil, and limited upward gaze on the left. His blood pressure is 85/55 mmHg with a heart rate of 110 bpm. | Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|

| Sudden severe headache ("worst ever") | Subarachnoid or meningeal irritation from blood in the sellar region and surrounding cisterns |

| Fatigue and weakness in recent weeks | Chronic hypopituitarism (probably ACTH and TSH deficiency) that has been building – the apoplexy then unmasks acute decompensation |
| Obesity + acromegaly | Suggests pre-existing endocrine pathology that may have been poorly controlled or undiagnosed |

- **Isolated ophthalmoplegia without headache:** A painless third nerve palsy with pupil involvement (no subarachnoid bleeding, only cavernous sinus compression). Can be mistaken for a posterior communicating artery aneurysm if you don't check for hypotension.
- **Nonspecific collapse with encephalopathy:** GCS 8, no localising signs. The hypotension is treated with fluids but they don't improve. You forget to check cortisol and give steroids – patient crashes when you start thyroid hormone replacement (untreated adrenal insufficiency ➜ precipitates adrenal crisis).
- **Fever + neck stiffness + photophobia:** Looks like meningitis or subarachnoid haemorrhage. Lumbar puncture shows xanthochromia due to blood breakdown products. But the real clue is the low sodium (hyponatraemia from secondary adrenal insufficiency or SIADH) and hypotension unresponsive to antibiotics.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|-----------|---------------|
| Haemorrhage into tumour → sudden sella expansion + irritation of meninges | **Severe headache** (worse than any previous, often occipital or retro-orbital) – from meningeal irritation (similar to SAH) |
| Compression of optic chiasm | **Bitemporal hemianopia** – classically with a central scotoma; can progress to blindness |
| Lateral expansion into cavernous sinus → cranial nerves III, IV, V1, V2, VI compromised | **Ptosis, mydriasis, upward gaze palsy** (III), **ophthalmoplegia** (IV, VI), **periorbital numbness** (V1, V2) |
| Loss of ACTH → acute secondary adrenal insufficiency | **Hypotension, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, shock** – cortisol absent means no permissive effect on catecholamines, no gluconeogenesis, no sodium retention |
| Loss of TSH → acute secondary hypothyroidism | **Fatigue, cold intolerance, bradycardia** – but less immediate because T4 has long half-life; however, giving T4 without cortisol can worsen adrenal crisis |
| Possible compression of hypothalamic-pituitary stalk → loss of ADH | **Diabetes insipidus** (polyuria, polydipsia, hypernatraemia) – less common but can be missed |

- The **fatigue and weakness** prodrome reflects chronic hypopituitarism – the apoplexy is the acute-on-chronic decompensation.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

Most cases are **spontaneous** in a previously undiagnosed adenoma (often null-cell or prolactinoma). But there are identifiable triggers:

| Category | Trigger / Cause | Stem Clue That Points to It |
|----------|----------------|-----------------------------|
| **Most common** | Spontaneous haemorrhage into pre-existing adenoma (often non-functioning) | Patient with no known diagnosis, presents with headache + visual loss; no trigger found |
| **Iatrogenic** | Anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs, heparin) | Patient on blood thinners for AF, VTE, or mechanical valve; sudden headache |
| **Iatrogenic** | Surgery (e.g., hip replacement, CABG) – hypotension + bleeding tendency | Post-operative day 1–3, patient develops headache and hypotension; may be attributed to anaesthetic or fluid shift |
| **Endocrine** | Pregnancy (Sheehan's syndrome variant) – in a gravid woman with known pituitary adenoma | Pregnant woman with headache and hypotension; rare but possible |
| **Drug-induced** | Dopamine agonists (bromocriptine, cabergoline) – can cause tumour necrosis | Known prolactinoma treated with dopamine agonist; rapid shrinkage can precipitate apoplexy |
| **Trauma** | Head injury with direct pituitary trauma | Patient with recent MVA or fall; may have other skull base fractures |
| **Radiation** | Radiotherapy to sellar region (e.g., for tumour) | Headache months after pituitary radiation |
| **Infectious / Inflammatory** | Very rare – can be associated with polyarteritis nodosa, SLE, or acute infection | Unusual context; look for systemic vasculitis features |

**Highlight the most common cause:** Spontaneous haemorrhage into a preexisting non-functioning pituitary adenoma.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria:**  
There is no formal consensus scoring system, but a clinical diagnosis is based on **sudden onset severe headache + visual disturbance + features of hypopituitarism** combined with imaging confirmation.

**Key Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Urgent non-contrast CT head (sella protocol)** | Hyperdense mass in the sella (acute haemorrhage) with possible extension into suprasellar cistern or cavernous sinus | First-line imaging – fast, widely available, can show acute haemorrhage as high density | **Positive:** hyperdensity in sella confirms haemorrhage. **Negative does not rule out** – infarct without haemorrhage may be isodense. Need MRI. |
| **MRI brain with contrast (pituitary protocol)** | T1 hyperintensity in acute/subacute haemorrhage; T2 hypointensity; may show tumour + fluid levels; contrast enhancement of viable tumour | Gold standard – better for differentiating haemorrhage vs infarction, and for surgical planning | **Single finding that confirms:** Heterogeneous mass in sella with evidence of recent haemorrhage and compression of optic chiasm. |
| **Serum cortisol (random, or 8am)** | Low cortisol (< 100 nmol/L or < 3.6 μg/dL) in acute setting | Diagnose adrenal crisis – do NOT wait for synacthen test; treat empirically | **Critical:** a low cortisol in a hypotensive patient with headache is diagnostic of adrenal insufficiency. **Do not delay steroids for this result** – give hydrocortisone first. |
| **Serum ACTH** | Low or inappropriately normal (should be high in response to low cortisol) | Distinguish primary from secondary adrenal insufficiency | Low ACTH + low cortisol = secondary (pituitary) cause. |
| **Serum prolactin** | Highly elevated in stalk compression (disconnection); or low in pituitary destruction | Helps differentiate apoplexy from prolactinoma – if prolactin > 5000 mIU/L, suggests prolactinoma with apoplexy (medical treatment may be preferred) | **Stalk effect:** moderate elevation (1000–3000). > 5000 suggests pure prolactinoma. |
| **Full pituitary panel** | LH, FSH, TSH, free T4, GH, IGF-1 | Assess baseline deficits for long-term replacement | May be low or low-normal; insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) may be low if GH deficient. |
| **Electrolytes** | Hyponatraemia (low Na < 135) | Adrenal insufficiency causes impaired free water excretion | Moderate to severe hyponatraemia (Na 115–130) is common. |
| **Blood glucose** | Hypoglycaemia (low glucose < 3.3 mmol/L) | Adrenal insufficiency causes fasting hypoglycaemia | Can be severe; gives clue to adrenal crisis in a collapsed patient. |
| **Coagulation screen** | PT/APTT/INR if on anticoagulants | Identify iatrogenic trigger | Elevated INR if warfarin; normal in spontaneous cases. |

**Pattern-Recognition: What Single Finding Confirms / Rules Out?**

- **Confirms:** MRI showing intrasellar haemorrhage with optic chiasm compression + low random cortisol in a shocked patient.
- **Rules out:** Normal cortisol (> 500 nmol/L) in a hypotensive patient makes adrenal crisis unlikely (but does not rule out apoplexy itself – could have infarct without complete ACTH loss; still need imaging).

**Note on Lumbar Puncture:**  
Only perform if CT negative and you suspect SAH. In apoplexy, CSF may be xanthochromic or bloody, but this is not specific. Do not tap if there is suspicion of raised ICP (focal signs, altered GCS) – risk of herniation.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (First Hour):**

1. **Airway, Breathing, Circulation** – High-flow O₂, two large-bore IVs. If GCS ≤ 8, intubate.
2. **Bloods** – Random cortisol, ACTH, prolactin, full pituitary profile, U&Es, glucose, coagulation, FBC.
3. **Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV STAT** – This is the single most important drug. Do not wait for cortisol result. In adrenal crisis, cortisol deficiency is immediately life-threatening.
4. **Fluid resuscitation** – Start with 0.9% saline (or dextrose saline if hypoglycaemic). Avoid hypotonic fluids (worsens hyponatraemia).
5. **Correct hypoglycaemia** – If glucose < 3.3, give 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV, then 5% dextrose infusion.
6. **Stat CT head** (sella protocol) – to identify haemorrhage and plan for neurosurgery.
7. **Discuss with neurosurgery** – Even if you aren't sure of the diagnosis, any suspected apoplexy warrants neurosurgical consultation.

**What NOT To Do:**
- **Do not give levothyroxine before hydrocortisone** – this can precipitate adrenal crisis because T4 increases metabolic clearance of cortisol.
- **Do not give desmopressin for hyponatraemia** – the hyponatraemia is from adrenal insufficiency, not SIADH. Steroids will correct it.
- **Do not give mannitol for raised ICP** – unless confirmed by CT; steroids may help.

**Ward (24–72 Hours):**

- **Hydrocortisone 50 mg IV QDS** (or 100 mg TDS) – continue as stress dose until stable, then taper to oral replacement.
- **Monitor:**
  - **Vital signs q15 min** – BP, HR, GCS, pupils.
  - **Visual fields and acuity daily** – documented by neurosurgery or ophthalmology.
  - **Serum sodium and glucose q6h** – to guide fluids and dextrose.
- **MRI pituitary** – within 24 hours for definitive diagnosis and surgical planning.
- **Neurosurgical decompression (transsphenoidal)** – indicated if:
  - **Progressive visual loss** (not improving with steroids)
  - **Severe ophthalmoplegia** with no improvement
  - **Coma or decreasing GCS** due to mass effect
  - **Large haemorrhage** compressing hypothalamus
- **If no surgery** (e.g., mild symptoms, improving vision), continue steroids and observe with daily MRI.
- **Thyroid replacement** – only start after 24–48 hours of adequate steroid therapy. Start levothyroxine 50–100 mcg PO/NG daily (low dose to avoid metabolic crisis).

**Long-Term:**

- **Check full pituitary function at 6–12 weeks** – many patients develop permanent hypopituitarism.
- **Lifelong replacement:**
  - Hydrocortisone 10–20 mg PO daily in divided doses (e.g., 10 mg on waking, 5 mg at 4 pm)
  - Levothyroxine 75–150 mcg daily (titrate to free T4 in upper normal range)
  - Sex hormone replacement if needed (testosterone / oestrogen)
  - Growth hormone (if deficient and no contraindication) after 1 year
- **Diabetes insipidus** – if present, treat with desmopressin (DDAVP) 100–200 mcg PO TDS or 1–2 mcg SC.
- **Follow-up imaging** – MRI at 3, 6, 12 months then yearly for 3 years to monitor for tumour regrowth.
- **Sick day rules** – corticosteroid dose doubling during illness to prevent adrenal crisis (teach patient and family).

**Drug Doses Inline:**

- Hydrocortisone: 100 mg IV stat, then 50 mg IV QDS for 24–72h, then oral taper.
- Levothyroxine: 50–100 mcg PO daily, start after 24h of steroids.
- Desmopressin: 100–200 mcg PO TDS (or 1–2 mcg SC/IV) as needed for DI.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Pituitary Apoplexy | Subarachnoid Haemorrhage (SAH) | Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis | Meningitis | Migraine (Severe) |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------|------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | Sudden, severe, retro-orbital or occipital | Thunderclap headache (worst ever), often occipital/nuchal | Gradual onset, periorbital pain with redness | Diffuse, with neck pain | Unilateral, throbbing, may have aura |
| **Exam** | Ptosis, mydriasis, abducens palsy; bitemporal hemianopia; hypotension; pallor | Neck stiffness, photophobia, focal signs less common (unless aneurysm compressing CN III – then pupil usually dilated) | Proptosis, chemosis, periorbital oedema; CN III, IV, VI palsy; fever; no hypotension | Fever, meningism, altered GCS, rash (N. meningitidis) | May have photophobia, but no focal signs; no hypotension |
| **ECG** | Non-specific (may show sinus tachycardia from shock) | May show ST-T changes due to neurogenic stunned myocardium | Sinus tachycardia from sepsis | Tachycardia from fever | Normal |
| **Labs** | Low cortisol, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia; prolactin may be high or low; coagulopathy if on anticoagulants | Xanthochromia / blood on LP; normal cortisol | ↑ WCC, ↑ CRP, may have positive blood cultures | ↑ WCC, ↑ CRP; LP shows pleocytosis and low glucose | Normal |
| **Key differentiating feature** | **Low cortisol + hypotension + visual field defect** | **LP with xanthochromia** + thunderclap headache without endocrine features | **Fever + proptosis + chemosis** + cavernous sinus signs; no adrenal crisis | **Fever + meningism**; no ocular motor palsy unless advanced; normal cortisol | **Recurrent pattern**; normal exam and labs between attacks; no visual field loss |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Adrenal crisis with cardiovascular collapse** | Profound hypotension unresponsive to fluids, hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, lethargy → coma | BP < 90/60, HR > 120, GCS < 12, glucose < 3.3, Na < 130 | Give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat; fluids; treat cause. Move to ICU. |
| **Permanent visual loss** | Progressive bitemporal hemianopia → blindness over hours to days | No light perception; optic atrophy develops after 2 weeks if untreated | Urgent neurosurgical decompression. Vision can be saved if treated within 48–72 hours. |
| **Permanent cranial nerve palsy** | Persistent ptosis, diplopia, ophthalmoplegia | CN III, IV, VI deficits persist beyond 6–12 months | Decompression may help if early; later – squint surgery. |
| **Permanent panhypopituitarism** | Lifelong dependence on corticosteroids, thyroid, sex hormones, GH | Fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, cold intolerance, infertility, loss of libido | Lifelong hormone replacement; teach sick day rules; endocrine follow-up. |
| **Diabetes insipidus** | Polyuria > 3 L/day, polydipsia, hypernatraemia (Na > 145) | Urine output > 200 mL/h, thirst, high serum osmolality | Desmopressin; monitor Na carefully; may be temporary or permanent. |
| **Recurrent apoplexy** | Regrowth of tumour with repeat haemorrhage | Same presentation weeks to months later | Consider radiotherapy if residual tumour; long-term MRI surveillance. |

**Late Presentation Pattern (What Happens If Untreated):**  
A patient found unconscious with fixed dilated pupils bilaterally, no eye movements, and refractory hypotension. They may have had a mild headache days ago that was ignored. Their GCS is 3, they are ventilated, and CT shows massive sella haematoma with hydrocephalus. Prognosis is grim – they likely will not regain consciousness and may die from brain herniation.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **PITUITARY APOPLEXY** (for key features)  
   **P** – Pupil (CN III palsy with mydriasis)  
   **I** – Intense headache  
   **T** – TSH / ACTH loss (adrenal and thyroid)  
   **U** – Unconsciousness / altered GCS  
   **I** – Ischaemic infarction of adenoma  
   **T** – Temporal field loss  
   **A** – Adrenal crisis (low cortisol)  
   **R** – Radiological (CT/MRI)  
   **Y** – Yield to steroids early  

   **A** – ACTH (low)  
   **P** – Prolactin (stalk effect)  
   **O** – Ocular palsies  
   **P** – Pituitary tumour  
   **L** – Low sodium  
   **E** – Emergency steroid  
   **X** – X-ray (CT)  
   **Y** – Yes, call neurosurgeon

2. **"Headache + Eye Signs + Shock"** — the triad you must never forget.

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**

- **Rule of thumb for hypotension:** If a patient with severe headache has BP < 90/60, check a random cortisol and give steroids before doing anything else.
- **Visual fields:** Apoplexy classically causes bitemporal hemianopia, but sometimes a central scotoma is the first sign – test red desaturation (a red pin disappears in the affected field).
- **Lumbar puncture crux:** If you suspect SAH but CT is negative, don't forget to check cortisol and prolactin before LP – a single low cortisol in a shocked patient is diagnostic.
- **The mimic of 'sepsis':** Pituitary apoplexy can present with fever and tachycardia – don't be fooled into treating as sepsis without giving steroids.
- **Don't start thyroxine:** Until you have given hydrocortisone – this is a classic trap in ICU.
- **Prolactin level:** A very high prolactin (> 5000 mIU/L) suggests a prolactinoma; if the patient has apoplexy, medical therapy (bromocriptine) may be first line instead of surgery, but always discuss with endocrinology.
- **Pregnancy:** In a pregnant woman with headache and hypotension, consider Sheehan's syndrome (postpartum infarction) – but apoplexy can occur in any trimester if there is an adenoma.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**

- Giving IV levothyroxine before steroids (precipitates hypotension)
- Attributing hypotension to sepsis without checking cortisol
- Ordering lumbar puncture when there is a known sellar mass (risk of coning)
- Using desmopressin for hyponatraemia without first correcting cortisol deficiency
- Discharging a patient with mild apoplexy without endocrine follow-up (they will relapse)

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to Call Senior / ICU / Neurosurgery:**

| Condition | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Hypotension unresponsive to 500 mL fluid bolus | BP < 90/60 after 1L saline | Call senior (registrar or consultant) and prepare ICU transfer – may need vasopressors. |
| GCS < 13 or falling by ≥ 2 points in 30 minutes | GCS 8 or below = consider intubation | Call crash team / anaesthetics; intubate for airway protection and CT head. |
| New focal neurological deficit (visual loss, CN palsy) | Any visual field loss or new fixed dilated pupil | Urgent neurosurgery consult – may need decompression within hours. |
| Severe hyponatraemia (Na < 120) with seizures | Na < 120 or seizure | Call ICU; give hypertonic saline (3%) 100 mL over 10 minutes if seizure, then endocrinology input. |
| Hypoglycaemia (glucose < 2.2) not correcting with 50% dextrose | Persistent hypoglycaemia after 2 ampules | Add hydrocortisone if not already given; inform senior. |
| Signs of transtentorial herniation (unequal pupils, decerebrate posturing, Cushing's reflex) | Any one of these | Crash call – emergent neurosurgery; consider mannitol (but steroids first); prepare for theatre. |
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥ 7 (especially if component for consciousness) | Escalate to critical care outreach; organise CT. |

**Specific Thresholds to Document:**

- Δ GCS ≥ 2 from baseline
- SBP < 90 mmHg despite 1L crystalloid
- Lactate > 4 mmol/L
- Na < 125 or > 150
- Glucose < 3.0 mmol/L
- PaO₂ < 8 kPa on air (or SpO₂ < 92% on 15 L O₂)
- Any seizure

**Remember:** If you suspect pituitary apoplexy, **call the neurosurgery registrar and the endocrinology registrar immediately** – even before confirmatory imaging. This is a time-critical joint medical–surgical emergency.
